explanation:
Proper **drainage design** is critical in construction: concrete foundations, rebar, brick, stucco, waterproofing, and drain systems. Building codes and standards ensure systems function correctly.

## How it works

This calculator looks up the recommended grate diameter based on your fixture type, checking against typical industry ranges and alerting you if values seem unusual.

## Important notes

This calculation is a reference guide only. For critical decisions (structural, plumbing code compliance, building permits), consult a licensed plumber or engineer. All values are current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.q: What size drain grate do I need for a sink?
faq.a: Standard sink drains use 50 mm (2") grates. Kitchen sinks often use 75–100 mm (3–4") for better drainage.

faq.q: What are standard US drain grate sizes?
faq.a: Typical ranges: 50 mm for sinks, 75–100 mm for kitchens and showers, 110–150 mm for patio and roof drains.

faq.q: What materials are used for drain grates?
faq.a: Common materials: plastic ABS (most affordable), PVC, aluminum, and bronze for durability.

faq.q: What is a P-trap and is it required?
faq.a: A P-trap holds standing water to block sewer gases and odors. It's required by building code on every drain.

faq.q: What slope should drain pipes have?
faq.a: Proper slope is 1–2% (1/8" to 1/4" per foot) to ensure water flows smoothly without pooling.

faq.q: How much roof runoff can a 100 mm drain handle?
faq.a: A 100 mm grate typically handles rainfall for roof areas up to 50 square meters in moderate climates.
